title: Print a message
summary: Start with the smallest possible win: make Python print some text.
why: Printing is the fastest way to see that your code is doing something.
teach: Python runs one line at a time. The print() function shows text in the output area. Text needs quote marks around it.
brief: Make the program print exactly: Hello, Python!
test_code:
output = stdout_buffer.getvalue().strip()
assert output == 'Hello, Python!', f'expected Hello, Python! but got {output!r}'
hints: The text should be inside quotes.
Match the output exactly: Hello, Python!

title: Store a name in a variable
summary: Save a value in a variable, then use it.
why: Variables let you remember information and reuse it later.
teach: A variable is like a labelled box. Put a value in the box with =, then use the box name later.
brief: Create a variable called name with the value Charlie, then print it.
test_code:
assert 'name' in namespace, 'create a variable called name'
assert namespace['name'] == 'Charlie', 'name should be Charlie'
output = stdout_buffer.getvalue().strip()
assert output == 'Charlie', f'expected Charlie but got {output!r}'
hints: Variable names do not need quotes. String values do.

title: Add two numbers
why: Most programming starts with combining values and storing the result.
teach: Python can add numbers with +. You can store the answer in a variable and print it.
brief: Create a variable called total that adds 7 + 5, then print the result.
test_code:
assert 'total' in namespace, 'create a variable called total'
assert namespace['total'] == 12, 'total should be 12'
output = stdout_buffer.getvalue().strip()
assert output == '12', f'expected 12 but got {output!r}'

title: Fix a broken print
summary: Learn what a tiny syntax mistake looks like and fix it.
why: Beginners hit syntax errors constantly. Seeing one early makes them less scary.
teach: Syntax means the grammar rules of Python. If punctuation is missing, the program stops before it can run.
brief: Fix the code so it prints exactly: Ready to learn
test_code:
output = stdout_buffer.getvalue().strip()
assert output == 'Ready to learn', f'expected Ready to learn but got {output!r}'
hints: The text itself is already correct.

title: Make a simple if statement
summary: Run code only when a condition is true.
why: Conditions are how programs make choices.
teach: An if statement checks something. If it is true, the indented code underneath runs. Python blocks end with a colon.
brief: Set is_sunny to True and write an if statement that prints Wear sunglasses.
starter_code:
is_sunny = True
# write your if statement below
test_code:
assert namespace.get('is_sunny') is True, 'is_sunny should be True'
output = stdout_buffer.getvalue().strip()
assert output == 'Wear sunglasses', f'expected Wear sunglasses but got {output!r}'
hints: The next line must be indented.

title: Repeat with a loop
summary: Make Python repeat an action several times.
why: Loops save you from writing the same thing again and again.
teach: A for loop can repeat code. range(3) means three turns: 0, 1, 2. You often ignore the number at first and just repeat the action.
brief: Write a loop that prints Hi three times.
test_code:
output = stdout_buffer.getvalue().strip().splitlines()
assert output == ['Hi', 'Hi', 'Hi'], f'expected Hi three times but got {output!r}'
hints: The print line must be indented.
